---
title: "Parallelization in AlphaSimR"
author: "Gregor Gorjanc and Chris Gaynor"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Parallelization in AlphaSimR}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

## Introduction

`AlphaSimR` leverages `OpenMP`-type parallelization to speed-up
execution of some underlying `C++` code called by `R` code. This
speed-up can be substantial for large-scale simulations. Depending on
the version and source of your installed `R` and `AlphaSimR`, `OpenMP`
might or might not be supported out of the box. In our experience, it is
usually available automatically on Windows and Linux. macOS users most
often need to take extra steps. We show below how to check if you can
use multiple threads and how these get used in `AlphaSimR`. We also
point to more information on installation/building to enable `OpenMP`.

## Check whether OpenMP is available

The simplest check is via `getNumThreads()`.

```{r}
library(AlphaSimR)
getNumThreads()
```

If `OpenMP` is not available (or when you only have one available
thread), `getNumThreads()` returns `1` and all code will run
single-threaded. When `getNumThreads()` returns more than `1`, some code
will run in parallel.

## Controlling the number of threads

When `OpenMP` is enabled, `AlphaSimR` uses all the available threads by
default. You can control the number of threads used via the `nThreads`
argument globally in the `SP` object or individually in some functions.

```{r}
founderPop = quickHaplo(nInd = 4, nChr = 2, segSites = 5)
SP = SimParam$new(founderPop)
SP$nThreads
```

Assuming that you have more than one thread, but want to use just one
globally, you can specify that in the `SP` object:

```{r}
pop = newPop(founderPop, simParam = SP)
SP$nThreads = 1L
pullSegSiteGeno(pop)
# Note that pullSegSiteGeno() internally uses SP$nThreads,
```

## A demonstration

There are several `AlphaSimR` functions that leverage parallelization,
particularly when working across chromosomes or with linear algebra
operations. `runMacs()` is one such example. The code below reports time
for simulating genomes with 10 chromosomes for 50 individuals in serial
mode and in parallel mode. You can evaluate the speed-up due to
parallelization by changing the number of chromosomes, and also
increasing the size of simulation by increasing the number of
individuals.

```{r}
system.time(runMacs(nInd = 50, nChr = 10, nThreads = 1L))
system.time(runMacs(nInd = 50, nChr = 10, nThreads = getNumThreads()))
```

## How to get OpenMP support

This topic depends on your platform and `R` toolchain, so we keep the
guidance short and point to the relevant docs:

- Windows and Linux: in our experience, `OpenMP` is usually enabled
  automatically. Install `AlphaSimR` and check
  `AlphaSimR::getNumThreads()`.

- macOS: if `OpenMP` is not already available, follow the instructions
  at <https://mac.r-project.org/openmp/>, then reinstall `AlphaSimR`
  from the `CRAN` or `GitHub` source, and then check
  `AlphaSimR::getNumThreads()`.

## A note on `BLAS`

For linear algebra-heavy work (such as fitting genomic prediction
models), a multithreaded `BLAS` can also help. This is separate to
`OpenMP` support. In our experience, this is even more platform- and
`R`-build-specific than `OpenMP`, so it is best treated as an advanced
local optimisation.

- Windows: We have had reasonable results with `OpenBLAS`:
  <https://github.com/OpenMathLib/OpenBLAS>.

- Linux: `BLAS` setup depends on how `R` was installed. You may be able
  to replace the shared `BLAS` library, or compile `R` against a
  specific `BLAS`. See the `R Installation and Administration` manual:
  <https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-admin.html#BLAS>.

- macOS: see the `R for macOS FAQ`:
  <https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/RMacOSX-FAQ.html#Which-BLAS-is-used-and-how-can-it-be-changed_003f>.
  On recent `R` versions, simple `BLAS` library swapping may not work
  cleanly, so follow the guidance for your specific `R` build and verify
  that base `R` still starts correctly after any change.
